text: The surgeon incises along the white line of Toldt, mobilizing the right colon medially to facilitate the removal of disease below the diaphragm.
The surgeon mobilizes the duodenum to expose the right renal hilum, followed by excision of tissue over the inferior vena cava.
The surgeon highlights the gonadal vein with a green arrow, while the ureter is also marked. The psoas muscle is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon retracts the ureter superiorly and continues dissection along the anterior aspect of the inferior vena cava. The renal vein is visible, and the renal artery is clamped and divided using a laparoscopic vascular stapler.

An ultrasound probe is used to delineate the extent of the renal vein thrombus, which extends to the vena cava. A suture is placed around the renal vein to facilitate the upward manipulation of the thrombus. A clamp is positioned below the suture, and a stick tie is applied to secure the renal vein, providing venous control with both the clip and suture visible.

A second clip is placed on the renal vein, which is then opened to visualize the lumen and confirm the absence of thrombus. The lumen appears clean. Dissection proceeds along the anterior surface of the inferior vena cava (IVC), with the overlying tissue being divided to reach the adrenal vein level. The adrenal gland is carefully separated from the vena cava.

The surgeon clips and divides the adrenal vein using a harmonic scalpel. Superior dissection is performed to mobilize the kidney, while lateral tissue is divided to achieve complete mobilization of the kidney.

The surgeon places the kidney specimen into a fifteen millimeter endokitch bag for later retrieval. Subsequently, lymph node dissection is performed, with the vena cava fully mobilized from surrounding lymphatic tissue.
The surgeon highlights the renal vein on the left side and proceeds to divide the lymphatic package, which is part of the pericaval lymph nodes.

The surgeon fully mobilizes a nodal packet and places it in a ten millimeter endo-catch bag. Additional peri-cable and retro-cable lymph nodes are mobilized, with lymphatic tissue being divided after clipping.

The surgeon dissects pericable lymph nodes around the renal hilum, removing bulky lymphatic tissue. The dissection follows a template similar to that used for testicular RPLND, ensuring all visible tissue seen on the CT scan is excised.

The surgeon mobilizes lymph node packets and places them in a fifteen millimeter endocatch bag for later delivery. The left renal vein is exposed, and the aorta is highlighted with a green arrow, with surrounding tissue being exposed.

The surgeon exposes the right renal artery in the interaerial cable space while the inferior vena cava (IVC) is retracted superiorly, employing a technique similar to the split and roll method used in testicular RPLND.

The surgeon highlights a bulky retrocaval lymph node and the pulsating right renal artery in the interaerial cable space. Dissection is performed to separate the lymph node from the right renal artery, while a Kittner is used to retract the vena cava superiorly for better access. The large lymph node packet is removed and prepared for placement in an endocatch bag.
The surgeon continues the dissection inferiorly, carefully avoiding injury to the common iliac vessels as the aorta and vena cava bifurcate.
The surgeon dissects the inferior-most lymph node identified on the CT scan, carefully clipping the lymphatic tissue before cutting to prevent chyle and lymph leaks.

The surgeon performs node dissection involving the right kidney and the left aspect of the aorta. Dense adhesions of lymphatic tissue to the vena cava are visible, requiring a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques to separate the nodal tissue from the vena cava.

The surgeon examines the pericaval space, revealing the inner aorta cable space with the aorta visible. The right renal artery is highlighted, and the vena cava is superiorly retracted. No nodal tissues are observed in the area.
